label: argentina-france-di-maria
title: Di María 36' (Argentina v France, final)
summary: Argentina build the third goal from Tagliafico's interception. Watch attention follow Mac Allister and Di María through the chain — and how P(score) climbs as the defence is spread before any pass on the goal is made.

moves.label: Molina drifts onto the Tagliafico–Alvarez line
moves.narrative: The third-man triangle says C's value is in being behind the defenders' eye-line. Pull Molina 5 m onto the Tagliafico–Alvarez passing lane and the first-time release disappears — the off-ball geometry is doing the work.

moves.label: Tagliafico doesn't make the run
moves.narrative: Hold Tagliafico 12 m back instead of sprinting into the channel. The cleanest off-ball chemistry test we have: if the lane closes for everyone else, the run was load-bearing.

moves.label: Alvarez drops 4 m further from Tagliafico
moves.narrative: If Alvarez is too far, the bounce loses pace and the third-man release to Molina never happens. The model's P(score) tells you how tight the triangle has to be.

moves.label: Tagliafico pinches narrow
moves.narrative: The pin says Tagliafico's threat is fixing the fullback wide. Pinch them 6 m central and the fullback no longer has a reason to stay outside — the channel reopens for the opposite side.

moves.label: Alvarez pushes 10 m higher
moves.narrative: Free-form perturbation (no named mechanism). Surface this if the curated mechanism moves all fall below the |Δ net| floor.

moves.label: Alvarez drops 10 m deeper

label: argentina-france-mbappe-volley
title: Mbappé 81' volley (France v Argentina, final)
summary: Mbappé's second goal in 97 seconds, this one off the volley. Watch P(concede) for Argentina spike as France break in transition before the strike.

moves.label: Messi arrives 10 m late to the press
moves.narrative: Move Messi 10 m further from the ball at the turnover. If the opponent's escape probability jumps, swarm compactness was the chemistry — not any one tackler.

moves.narrative: Hold Varane 12 m back instead of sprinting into the channel. The cleanest off-ball chemistry test we have: if the lane closes for everyone else, the run was load-bearing.

label: netherlands-usa-memphis
title: Memphis 10' (Netherlands v USA, R16)
summary: Netherlands' opener. Memphis at the end of a 20-pass Dutch sequence.

moves.label: Dijk pushes 10 m higher (no rest-defense anchor)
moves.narrative: Pull Dijk out of the rest-defense slot, 10 m higher. Watch P(concede) climb — the anchor was deterring a counter even without touching the ball. That's chemistry as *prevention*.

moves.label: Gakpo starts 5 m off the pre-scanned position
moves.narrative: Slide Gakpo 5 m sideways at the start of the sequence. If the passer pre-scanned where the receiver *was* about to be, breaking that geometry should kill the through-ball.

moves.label: Depay drifts onto the Gakpo–Jong line
moves.narrative: The third-man triangle says C's value is in being behind the defenders' eye-line. Pull Depay 5 m onto the Gakpo–Jong passing lane and the first-time release disappears — the off-ball geometry is doing the work.

moves.label: Jong drops 4 m further from Gakpo
moves.narrative: If Jong is too far, the bounce loses pace and the third-man release to Depay never happens. The model's P(score) tells you how tight the triangle has to be.
